medplum/medplum#7880.body:
I ran into the following during my Medplum cluster setup on AWS:
- After INIT tool completes, check all parameters in AWS Systems Manager > Application Tools > Parameter Stores (make sure that all signing keys and stuff are there AFTER init completes - there should be 13). If any are missing, you may run into deployment issues.
- Validate SSL by going to AWS Certificate Manager and creating records in route53 for that cert (if not in us-east-1, ensure that you do this for both the api cert AND the 2 certs in us-east-1 for cloudfront) 
- if cert is never issued to the the domain (remains in pending status until timeout), then the domain is likely NOT hosted by AWS, so the cert will never be issued. 
	- In this case, you will need to configure the name servers on the hosted system to point to the 4 NS values in the route53 hosted zone
- If `app.[basedomain]` or `api.[basedomain]` are inaccessible from the browser:
	- ensure that the cloudfront distribution has access to the S3 Bucket - also check to see if you ran the 'update-bucket-policies' command to ensure bucket policies are set correctly
	- go to that hosted zone in Route53 and create the records for `app.[basedomain]` and `api.[basedomain]` manually 
		- `app` points to the cloudfront distribution 
		- `api` points to the load balancer

Docs need to be updated with these notes in case someone runs into these issues during setup

medplum/medplum#7931.body:
### Goal

Investigate and propose a migration plan from our current Digicert-based code signing process to **Microsoft Trusted Signing** (formerly Azure Code Signing). The primary objective is to eliminate the operational burden, security risks, and workflow disruption associated with managing traditional, expiring code signing certificates for the Medplum Agent Windows executable.

https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/products/trusted-signing

https://github.com/Azure/trusted-signing-action

### Problem Description

Our current Digicert code signing certificate for the Medplum Agent has **expired**, halting new Windows executable releases. The process of renewing and re-integrating the certificate (involving private key handling, secure storage in GitHub Actions, and `signtool.exe` configuration) is known to be time-consuming and cumbersome. Furthermore, storing the private key in our CI/CD environment, even protected, introduces a security risk that we can mitigate.

### Proposed Solution: Microsoft Trusted Signing

Microsoft Trusted Signing is a managed, cloud-based service that offers several key advantages over our current process:

* **Managed Certificate Lifecycle:** Microsoft handles the certificate and renewal process, eliminating our operational burden.
* **Enhanced Security:** The private signing key is stored securely in a **FIPS 140-2 Level 3 compliant Hardware Security Module (HSM)**, never leaving Microsoft's service.
* **Modern CI/CD Integration:** Supports secure **digest signing**, where only a hash of the executable is sent for signing, ensuring the executable file itself stays within our build environment.
* **Streamlined User Experience:** Signing with a Microsoft-managed root helps ensure immediate trust on Windows systems, reducing "Unknown Publisher" warnings for our hospital IT users.

### Investigation Tasks

The following tasks need to be completed to assess the viability and path for migration:

1.  **Understand Prerequisites & Validation:**
    * Determine the necessary **organization validation** steps required by Microsoft for a healthcare-focused company like Medplum.
    * Identify the **Azure subscription** requirements and associated costs for the service.
2.  **Authentication & Key Setup:**
    * Research the method for securely connecting our **GitHub Actions** runner to the Azure Trusted Signing service (likely using a Service Principal or OpenID Connect (OIDC)).
    * Document the steps to create and configure the signing key in the service.
3.  **Workflow Integration:**
    * Find or create a proof-of-concept (PoC) workflow that uses the Trusted Signing API or the provided SDK/CLI tool within a GitHub Actions environment.
    * Verify that the resulting signature is valid and correctly recognized by Windows.
4.  **Cost Analysis:**
    * Compare the annual cost of Trusted Signing (based on usage/service tier) with the historical cost of a traditional Digicert EV Code Signing certificate.
medplum/medplum#7931.title: Investigate Microsoft Trusted Signing for Code Signing Workflow

I noticed an issue when trying to use a `medplum.config.json` file alongside the full stack Docker Compose stack:

```bash
❯ MEDPLUM_CONFIG_PATH=./medplum.config.json docker compose up
[+] Running 3/3
 ✔ Container medplum-full-stack-redis-1           Running                                                                                                                                                   0.0s
 ✔ Container medplum-full-stack-postgres-1        Running                                                                                                                                                   0.0s
 ✔ Container medplum-full-stack-medplum-server-1  Recreated                                                                                                                                                 0.0s
WARN[0000] could not start menu, an error occurred while starting: Error while reading terminfo data:termbox: unsupported terminal
Attaching to medplum-app-1, medplum-server-1, postgres-1, redis-1
medplum-server-1  | Config file found, running with custom config
medplum-server-1  | {"level":"INFO","timestamp":"2025-12-07T20:28:57.625Z","msg":"Starting Medplum Server...","configName":"file:./medplum.config.json","version":"5.0.0"}
medplum-server-1  | Error: ENOENT: no such file or directory, open './medplum.config.json'
medplum-server-1  |     at readFileSync (node:fs:440:20)
medplum-server-1  |     at loadFileConfig (file:///usr/src/medplum/packages/server/dist/index.js:431:21)
medplum-server-1  |     at loadConfig (file:///usr/src/medplum/packages/server/dist/index.js:374:22)
medplum-server-1  |     at main (file:///usr/src/medplum/packages/server/dist/index.js:50656:24)
medplum-server-1  |     at file:///usr/src/medplum/packages/server/dist/index.js:50677:3
medplum-server-1  |     at ModuleJob.run (node:internal/modules/esm/module_job:377:25)
medplum-server-1  |     at async onImport.tracePromise.__proto__ (node:internal/modules/esm/loader:691:26)
medplum-server-1  |     at async asyncRunEntryPointWithESMLoader (node:internal/modules/run_main:101:5) {
medplum-server-1  |   errno: -2,
medplum-server-1  |   code: 'ENOENT',
medplum-server-1  |   syscall: 'open',
medplum-server-1  |   path: './medplum.config.json'
medplum-server-1  | }
dependency failed to start: container medplum-full-stack-medplum-server-1 is unhealthy
```

The issue appears to be that the `MEDPLUM_CONFIG_PATH` is intended to specify both:
- The path to a _local_ Medplum configuration file on the host machine AND
- The path to the Medplum configuration file within the container

This leads to a behavior where Docker cannot find the config file at `MEDPLUM_CONFIG_PATH` (i.e., `./medplum.config.json`) because this isn't the same location where it actually is placed within the container, which is `/usr/src/medplum/packages/server/medplum.config.json`

Here's a repro, if helpful: https://github.com/Drew-Daniels/medplum-full-stack-repro

You should just be able to run `MEDPLUM_CONFIG_PATH=./medplum.config.json docker compose up` within that repository and see the error.

medplum/medplum#8278.body:
In #7794, we made the `Hl7Client` return on application-level ACK by default since we believed this was more aligned with most use-cases for the `Hl7Client`, and probably for most use cases with `pushToAgent`. However, we realized some users are actually relying on the old behavior as they are sending only `CA`s without ever returning an application-level ACK, which causes `pushToAgent` to hang on newer versions of the agent.

Since this was technically a breaking change that occurred after a major version bump and not with it, and because this change was not broadcasted in advance, it seems reasonable to revert the change in behavior at the agent level, by making the `ReturnAckCategory.FIRST` the default value passed to `Hl7Client#sendAndWait` with the `pushMessage` method in the agent, and allow for overrides at both the `Device` level (via URL params) and also on a per-message basis via a new optional operation parameter, and `MedplumClient#pushToAgent` option.
medplum/medplum#8278.title: [Agent] Revert breaking change to `pushToAgent` return ACK behavior

medplum/medplum#8340.body:
If a WebSocket is closed due to `client.unsubscribeFromCriteria` being called (e.g. when a component calling `useSubscription` is unmounted), it will not be reconnected when `client.subscribeToCriteria` is called again in future (e.g. when the component is remounted).

### Steps to reproduce
(These steps reproduce the underlying issue by unmounting and remounting the component, however, it should also be reproducable by changing the criteria or props passed to `useSubscription`)
- mount a component which calls `useSubscription`
- you should see that a subscription has been created in Redis
- trigger the subscription criteria you passed to `useSubscription`
- you should see that the callback passed to `useSubscription` is called
- unmount that component, and wait for the debounce timeout (3 secs; this comes from the useSubscription hook)
- you should see that the subscription is no longer in Redis
- remount that component
- trigger the subscription criteria you passed to `useSubscription`

#### Expected behaviour
There is a new subscription in Redis and the callback you passed to `useSubscription` is called.

#### Actual behaviour
There is no new subscription in Redis and the callback passed to `useSubscription` is not called.

### Explanation
- When `client.unsubscribeFromCriteria` is called, if the criteria count is 0 on the `SubscriptionManager`, `this.subscriptionManager.closeWebSocket()` will be called. Manually closing the WebSocket sets `ws.shouldReconnect` to `false` (reconnecting-websocket.ts `close`).
- When the component calling `useSubscription` is remounted, and `client.subscribeToCriteria` is called, `subscriptionManager.subscribeToCriteria` will return early since `this.ws.readyState` is `WebSocket.CLOSED`, and therefore will not set up the subscription.

### Potential fix
In `client.subscribeToCriteria`, if the WebSocket of the SubscriptionManager is closed, call `reconnectWebSocket` before `addCriteria`.
medplum/medplum#8340.title: useSubscription - WebSocket does not reconnect after close (when criteria count reaches 0)

medplum/medplum#8345.body:
### Feature Request
Add the ability to search on `Task.restriction.period.start` 

### Background & Use Case
In our application, we are pre-creating tasks for patients according to their care plan. Many of these tasks are simple, in 3 weeks take this checkin survey, but some are more complicated. We are using `Task.restriction.period.start` to represent that the task is not ready/able to be started until a specific date. Aka, a "Not before" time. 

We would like to be able to search on this like due-date (`Task.restriction.period.end`) to be able to more efficiently operate on these tasks. This isn't a major problem on the Patient side since we're filtering to only the patient's tasks, but on the Provider side and in Bots we are ending up with large numbers of future tasks and finding the ones due soon/now can only be done in-memory
medplum/medplum#8345.title: Feature Request: Add "Not before" Search param to Tasks

medplum/medplum#8348.body:
## Problem

When using `@medplum/fhirtypes` with TypeScript's `moduleResolution: "nodenext"`, imports fail with errors like:

```
error TS2305: Module '"@medplum/fhirtypes"' has no exported member 'ExtractResource'.
error TS2305: Module '"@medplum/fhirtypes"' has no exported member 'Resource'.
```

## Cause

The package has `"type": "module"` in `package.json`, but the type declaration files use re-exports without file extensions:

```typescript
// dist/index.d.ts
export * from './ResourceType';
export * from './Resource';
```

With `moduleResolution: "nodenext"`, TypeScript follows strict Node.js ESM resolution rules which require explicit file extensions.

## Suggested Fix

Add `.js` extensions to the re-exports in the generated `.d.ts` files:

```typescript
export * from './ResourceType.js';
export * from './Resource.js';
```

The `.js` extension works correctly because TypeScript maps `.js` imports to `.d.ts` files during type resolution.

## Workaround

Users can work around this by using `moduleResolution: "bundler"` instead of `"nodenext"`, but this may not be suitable for all projects (e.g., those publishing ESM packages for Node.js).

## Environment

- `@medplum/fhirtypes`: 5.0.6
- TypeScript: 5.4.5
- Node.js: 22.x / 24.x
medplum/medplum#8348.title: Add explicit `type` keyword + `.d.ts` extensions to @medplum/fhirtypes type re-exports for strict `NodeNext` module resolution

medplum/medplum#8391.body:
## Summary

Currently, `tryExternalAuthLogin` in the external auth flow requires a `fhirUser` claim in the external JWT to identify the user's profile. This limits compatibility with external IDPs that don't include SMART-on-FHIR claims.

## Proposed Change

When no `fhirUser` claim is present in the external JWT, fall back to using the standard `sub` (subject) claim to look up a `ProjectMembership` by its `externalId` field.

### Flow

1. External JWT arrives at `/oauth2/userinfo`
2. Token is parsed, issuer is matched to a configured external auth provider
3. **Current behavior**: Extract `fhirUser` claim → find profile → find membership
4. **New behavior**: If no `fhirUser` claim, extract `sub` claim → search `ProjectMembership` where `externalId = sub` → resolve profile from membership

The `sub` claim is the standard JWT subject identifier (RFC 7519) and is present in virtually all JWTs. The `ProjectMembership.externalId` field is already supported and can be set during user invite. This creates a natural mapping: admin sets `externalId` on the membership to match the user's `sub` from the external IDP.

### Security

- Token validation via the IDP's userinfo endpoint remains unchanged
- The `externalId` is set by a project admin during invite, establishing a trusted binding
- Redis caching behavior is preserved


## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `tryExternalAuthLogin` falls back to `sub` claim when `fhirUser` is absent
- [ ] Membership is found by searching `ProjectMembership` where `externalId` matches `sub`
- [ ] Existing `fhirUser`-based flow continues to work unchanged
- [ ] Token validation via userinfo endpoint still occurs before membership lookup
- [ ] Unit tests cover: success via `sub`, missing `sub` when no `fhirUser`, `sub` with no matching membership
- [ ] Redis caching works correctly for sub-based lookups
medplum/medplum#8391.title: External auth: support sub claim lookup via ProjectMembership.externalId

medplum/medplum#8417.body:
eachDayOfInterval uses server-local date components instead of actor-local, affects $find and $book for evening queries
eachDayOfInterval in scheduling.ts:15-28 extracts date components via getFullYear()/getMonth()/getDate() (server-local) and passes them to ZonedDateTime.from() with the actor's timezone. On a UTC server with a US-timezone actor, the UTC date can be ahead of the actor's local date during evening hours.
Affected operations: $find and $book — both call resolveAvailability → eachDayOfInterval.
Trigger: interval.start falls between UTC midnight and actor's local midnight (~4pm PST / 7pm EST onward).
Impact: $find returns zero availability for narrow evening ranges. $book can reject valid evening appointments.

Existing tests don't exercise the crossover window.
medplum/medplum#8417.title: $find & $book time zone issues

medplum/medplum#8421.body:
## Overview
Added comprehensive documentation for the `useSubscription` hook lifecycle hooks (`onWebSocketOpen`, `onWebSocketClose`, `onSubscriptionConnect`, `onSubscriptionDisconnect`).

## What's Included
- Explanation of all four lifecycle hooks and when they're called
- Use cases for handling reconnection scenarios
- Best practices for mobile applications (React Native/Expo)
- Complete example showing how to catch up on missed events during reconnection
- Hook invocation order for different connection states

## Why This Matters
These lifecycle hooks are particularly useful for:
- Handling WebSocket disconnections (common when apps are backgrounded)
- Implementing "catch-up" mechanisms for missed events
- Providing user feedback about connection status
- Managing complex reconnection scenarios

This is especially important for mobile applications where network connectivity is frequently interrupted.
medplum/medplum#8421.title: Document useSubscription lifecycle hooks

medplum/medplum#8442.body:
Currently we needlessly send the resource that a subscription event fired for in every single entry for every single subscription on publish via Redis PubSub. This is extremely inefficient, since if there are even 10 subscriptions that fire for this resource, we copy the resource 10 times for every publish. This would take this from O(N) copies of the resource to O(1) for each resource that triggers multiple subscriptions.

NOTE: Since this does change the payload of the message the subscribing servers will be expecting, we will have to support a "v1" and "v2" payload temporarily.
medplum/medplum#8442.title: Optimization: factor out the resource for the subscription event so it's not in every entry

medplum/medplum#8473.body: Due to an edge case where a deploy job happens just late enough to not get collapsed into one deploy by the merge queue, you can actually get 2 deploys running concurrently, which is definitely dangerous. This is because our concurrency group is based on a key which will always be unique for every commit, which is not what we want. It should probably be based on the branch name (main). We should never see 2 deploy jobs running at the same time. See: These two deployments started concurrently and the later deployment actually finished first. I had to manually cancel the earlier deployment to stop it from clobbering the previous one.

medplum/medplum#8475.body:
Currently because of how we diff the changes present in the deployed commit, we often decide not to deploy in `cicd-deploy.sh` due to the diff since the last commit (`git diff --name-only HEAD HEAD~1`) not telling the full story for the merge group.

GitHub provides the SHA of the previous commit to an action via the context var `github.event.before`. We can use that to get the full diff for this deployment and correctly surgically deploy all the things that need to be deployed. 

**EDIT**: Looks like this `github.event.before` is actually only the last commit in this branch before this PUSH (meaning it will usually reference HEAD~1 unless you push multiple commits simultaneously, which we never do for `main`.

In order to truly make sure we get the full diff, I believe we may have to use the GitHub CLI to get the last sucessful Deploy action's commit SHA.
medplum/medplum#8475.title: Multiple commits in a single merge group can prevent server deployments

medplum/medplum#8635.body:
When submitting a large async batch, users can quickly consume their entire rate limit. However, they currently get a `429` Error when calling 
`/fhir/R4/job/:id/status` to _poll_ the rate limit status. 

## Desired Behavior
Checking the job status with a known id should bypass rate limits to allow clients to poll the status of ongoing async jobs

medplum/medplum#8642.body: Currently there is a possibility if a subscription is tracked in a user's subscription set that hasn't been cleaned up but should be garbage collected some time in the future, that the user is needlessly limited. We can attempt to cleanup any stale subscriptions upon hitting a user's limit and then recheck the limit before erroring out at Subscription creation.
medplum/medplum#8642.title: Attempt to cleanup stale subscriptions upon hitting user WebSocket sub max limit
